metadata.title: NSDI: A Digital Infrastructure for the Nation

sections.title: NSDI: A Digital Infrastructure for the Nation

elements.alt: A decorative line separating the title from the content

content.text: The NSDI is a digital infrastructure that connects location information from Federal, State, Tribal, and local Governments making that information available for all to use.
The NSDI connects national and Federal data to State, local, Tribal, and other data sources to deliver data for joint use, reuse, and informing local-to- national solutions.
Just like the Interstate Highway System (IHS), the NSDI is an infrastructure that enables every facet of our society.

elements.alt: Image showing a map of North America overlaid with a network of lines and nodes, representing seamless connection of local and national data.

content.text: … seamlessly connects local and national data.
The NSDI is the digital infrastructure that connects our Nation’s data enabling local to national solutions.

sections.title: What does the NSDI consist of?

content.text: The NSDI is made up of the
necessary to promote geospatial data sharing throughout the Federal Government, State, tribal, and local governments, non-profits, academia and the private sector.

elements.alt: Graphic illustrating how NSDI supports various activities across different sectors like Agriculture, Hazards, Infrastructure, Land & Infrastructure Management, and Transportation.

content.text: Location data is used across the Nation to address a wide variety of challenges critical to government and business – saving, time, money and lives.

sections.title: Examples of Local, State and Federal Data

content.text: Local boundaries (e.g., city, county, special service districts, school districts, and zoning designations)

Satellite remote sensing - Federal Domain for initial technology investment and continuity of operations, growing commercial space for use cases that can be monetized. No one is holding their breath for a state or local government funded satellite deployment.

sections.title: NSDI In Action Serving Citizens – North Carolina

content.text: Congress’s Bipartisan Infrastructure Law established the National Broadband Map to work with States to improve high-speed internet access to citizens.

North Carolina (NC) engaged the community through their NC OneMap portal and state SDI, providing integrated State and local geospatial data to test and map actual service levels, identifying 115,000 additional homes and businesses lacking high speed internet.
The result was increased service delivery that meets both the Federal intent and the actual needs of citizens and businesses.
The NSDI enables data access and sharing across Federal, State, local, and private sectors.

elements.alt: Map of the US showing broadband availability and a detailed map of NC Broadband Grants.

content.text: Emergencies large and small, including wildfires, floods, hurricanes, tornadoes, and earthquakes don’t respect administrative or political boundaries.

Roads, bridges, dams, electric and gas lines, telecom, sewer, water, and stormwater each have connection points that cross, or at least connect at, artificial administrative boundaries we’ve set up...cities, counties, states, even national boundaries.
We need to coordinate and collaborate across those boundaries. The data needs to facilitate that coordination and collaboration.

Everyday activities like getting an ambulance or fire truck to the right location or delivering packages or pizza to the right address, requires many people to work across our administrative boundaries.
How do we bring all these data together?
Our administrative boundaries, funding mechanisms, public policies and governance structures are set up in stovepipes
The NSDI is a mechanism to bring these data together to enable us to meet challenges of today and tomorrow.
Rethinking how to work together better.

Developing a national strategy that we're about to publish and developing several NSDI pilot projects that will help us learn more about how to work together more effectively across administrative boundaries.
Delivering the NSDI requires us to have complete, consistent and standardized across the administrative boundaries...nationwide.
Local and state governments use key national/NSDI data layers and contribute key content into nationwide layers.
Mature states like NC have an important role in contributing their experiences to establishing nationwide best practices (e.g., data governance) to help all this work better together.
Local/regional/states are all beneficiaries and contributors to the NSDI data (primarily through the state SDI),

sections.title: How are we going to get there?

content.text: Develop a shared vision and plan to get there

elements.alt: A chalkboard with 'Pop Quiz!' written on it.

sections.title: Vision 2035: Advancing the US National Spatial Data Infrastructure –

elements.text: A Strategic Plan for Innovation and Collaboration 2025 - 2035

content.text: Drafted initial set of goals based on stakeholder engagement

Solicited feedback from over 800 individuals and organizations (e.g., NGAC) through conferences (e.g., Esri FedUC and UC, URISA, Tribal GIS, NSGIC), webinars, meetings and one on one engagements to include many of you
Aligned it with United Nations Group of Experts on Global Geographic Information Management (UN GGIM) Integrated Geospatial information Framework (IGIF)
Integrated input from stakeholders and GeoGov Summit into actions in draft implementation plan

elements.alt: Table of Contents showing Introduction, Executive Summary, Vision, Mission, Core Values, Goals and Objectives, Use Cases, Future Outlook and Trends, Moving Forward, and Suggested Improvements.

content.text: A seamlessly interconnected national geospatial ecosystem.

Deliver highly responsive, timely, and dependable public and private geospatial data, applications and services that provide knowledge on-demand and actionable insights to address local, regional, national, and global challenges.
Governance: Implement National Governance and Collaboration
Data and Technology: Strengthen/Adapt the Infrastructure and Leverage Advanced Technology
People: Building a Skilled and Inclusive Geospatial Workforce for a Sustainable Future.

elements.caption: Draft NSDI Goals and Objectives matrix
elements.summary: Matrix mapping goals to specific objectives

elements.alt: FGDC.GOV FEDERAL GEOGRAPHIC DATA COMMITTEE logo
USGS science for a changing world logo

content.text: Establish a dynamic, inclusive, and collaborative governance and coordination structure where all sectors develop, contribute to, and implement a shared vision for the NSDI with a commitment to working together for the benefit of the Nation.
Refine the policy and legal framework to enable the NSDI.
Identify and meet financial and other resource needs necessary for implementing the NSDI Strategic Plan.

elements.alt: USGS science for a changing world logo

sections.title: Draft NSDI Goal 2: Data and Technology

content.text: Evaluate, improve, monitor, and advance the Nation's geospatial data portfolio and supporting infrastructure.
Prepare for, embrace, and promote rapid adoption of advancements in technology.
Create and maintain national standards that promote global interoperability and data sharing. Policies, strategies, and standards are key to a stable market.
Create an integrated geospatial platform, connecting systems of data with a highly efficient searchable index based on common standards of practice and enabling use of advanced technologies.
Foster multi-sectoral partnerships with tribal, federal, state, and local governments, private industry, academia, philanthropic, and non-profit organizations…
Access to a trained and educated geospatial workforce with the tools they need to deliver high-quality work.
Strengthen awareness and understanding of the NSDI and its values.
Input on NSDI Strategic Plan during public comment period
Call for nominations for National Geospatial Advisory Committee
Update and maintain data you have
Start thinking about your part in the strategic plan
How can we achieve the vision of the NSDI in 2035?
What actions are the responsibility of state and local governments?
